era_context: Darius I's Achaemenid Empire is at its height, consolidating administrative systems across three continents. In Greece, the early Classical period is emerging; Athens is stabilizing under democratic reforms while other city-states develop their own political systems. In China, the Spring and Autumn period continues with the state of Lu and other powers competing for influence. In India, the Mahajanapadas are forming as larger political units.

events.title: Darius I's Administrative Reorganization (ongoing)
events.description: Darius I continues the systematic reorganization of the Persian Empire into satrapies (provinces), each with a satrap (governor) responsible for taxation, military recruitment, and local administration. This period sees the formalization of the Royal Road system and standardized weights, measures, and coinage (the daric gold coin). These administrative innovations enable the empire to function as a coherent political entity despite vast distances and diverse populations.
events.certainty_note: The satrapy system is attested in contemporary Persian inscriptions and later Greek sources. The process was gradual, beginning after 522 BCE and continuing through Darius's reign.
cross_references: Part of broader administrative consolidation begun in -522

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events of major historical significance are reliably attested for 518 BCE. The administrative reorganization attributed to this period is a gradual process spanning years; no single-year turning point is documented. Modern scholarship treats the satrapy system as evolving over time rather than implemented in a single year.
historiographic_note: 518 BCE falls in a period of sparse specific documentation. Persian sources (inscriptions) provide general administrative information but rarely date events to specific years. Greek sources are retrospective and often unreliable for precise chronology. Chinese sources (Spring and Autumn Annals) are extremely terse for this period. Indian sources are largely absent from contemporary records. The year is essentially a gap in detailed historical documentation; it is included here to maintain chronological completeness, but no major events are securely dated to it.

graph_edges.note: Ongoing administrative consolidation of the Persian Empire
